figures.gloss: The founding master (1891-1943), a farmer's son from Yeonggwang in Jeolla province who, after years of self-directed searching, attained an awakening on 28 April 1916 without a teacher or lineage. Reading the Diamond Sutra afterward, he judged the Buddha-dharma the best frame for what he had realized — and then set his first disciples to building a savings union and reclaiming tidal land before he built them a doctrine.
figures.facet: A founder who arrives at Buddhism after his enlightenment rather than through it, and whose first sacred acts are economic — the levee at Yeonggwang stands in Won Buddhist memory where other traditions put a first sermon.
figures.provenance: The Scriptures of Won Buddhism (Wonbulgyo kyojon), trans. Bongkil Chung (2003), especially the Daejonggyeong discourses; Michael Pye, 'Won Buddhism as a Korean New Religion', Numen 49 (2002).

figures.gloss: The single black circle enshrined where other Buddhist halls place a Buddha statue. It stands for Il-Won, the unitary ultimate reality — identified in Won doctrine as the Dharmakaya Buddha, the mind-seal of all buddhas and sages, and the original nature of every sentient being.
figures.facet: Iconoclasm executed as an icon: the tradition keeps the devotional focal point but empties it of anthropomorphic form, so that worship is aimed past any person — including the historical Buddha — at the truth itself.
figures.provenance: The Principal Book (Jeongjeon) of the Wonbulgyo kyojon, trans. Bongkil Chung (2003); Bongkil Chung's doctrinal introduction.

figures.gloss: The signature Won practice: Son (Zen) meditation unfixed from the cushion and the mountain hall — 'at all times and in all places' — carried into farming, trade, housework, and speech. Paired with the maxim 'everywhere a Buddha image, every act a Buddha offering,' it makes daily life the monastery.
figures.facet: The laicization of the whole contemplative apparatus: rather than lowering the bar for householders, it relocates the entire discipline into their working day, dissolving the monastic/lay boundary from the practice side.
figures.provenance: The Principal Book (Jeongjeon), chapters on Timeless Son; trans. Bongkil Chung (2003).

figures.gloss: The doctrine that every existence is held in life by four graces — Heaven and Earth, Parents, Fellow Beings, and Laws — and that the religious debt owed for them is repaid not by ritual but by requital: protecting others as heaven protects, serving the helpless as parents served, and so on.
figures.facet: Dependent origination converted into an ethic of gratitude: interdependence is not merely observed, it is invoiced, turning metaphysics directly into social obligation.
figures.provenance: The Principal Book (Jeongjeon), Doctrine chapter; trans. Bongkil Chung (2003).

figures.gloss: Won Buddhism's founding motto: 'As material civilization develops (gaebyeok of matter), let us cultivate a corresponding Great Opening (gaebyeok) of spirit.' It reads the machine age itself as the epochal turn, and answers it not with retreat but with a modernization of the dharma to match.
figures.facet: The gaebyeok of Korea's founder religions with its polarity reversed: the Opening is not awaited from heaven but assigned to human self-cultivation, pacing spiritual work against industrial acceleration.
figures.provenance: Founding motto of the Wonbulgyo kyojon (trans. Bongkil Chung 2003); its place in the Korean gaebyeok lineage per Don Baker (2008) 'Korean Spirituality'.

edges.note: Direct reform-Buddhist descent: Won doctrine names Il-Won the original nature of all sentient beings — the Buddha-nature teaching inherited and set at the center of a modernized dharma.
The Principal Book identifies Il-Won-Sang explicitly with the Dharmakaya Buddha — the Truth Body of Trikaya doctrine drawn as a single circle.

Two answers to how dharma is transmitted: the Vajrayana root teacher absolutized as the sole living gate, against Sotaesan's deliberate laicization that opens the whole practice to everyone's working day.
The same gaebyeok expectation of Korea's founder-religion wave, attested in Won Buddhism's own founding motto — though Won reassigns the Opening from divine works to human self-cultivation.
Shared vocabulary of the same wave: the Great Opening that Donghak awaited and Jeungsanism declared accomplished becomes, in Won Buddhism, a program of spiritual modernization.

Era-kin founders of the same Jeolla-province religious ferment a decade apart — but inverted in self-claim: Kang declared himself the supreme god incarnate, Sotaesan claimed only an awakened human's authority.
Intra-tradition: the founder and the circle he set in the Buddha's place — his awakening given its aniconic seal.
Intra-tradition: Timeless Son is defined as keeping the mind aligned with Il-Won through every time and place — the practice is the circle walked.
Intra-tradition: the Fourfold Grace is Il-Won met concretely — the one reality encountered as the four debts that sustain a life.

dossier.essence: Won Buddhism (Wonbulgyo) is a Korean reformed Buddhism founded on the unaided awakening of Sotaesan (Pak Chung-bin, 1891-1943) on 28 April 1916 in Jeolla province; reading the Diamond Sutra afterward, he adopted the Buddha-dharma as the frame for what he had realized and set out to modernize it for lay life in an industrial age. Organized at Iksan in 1924 as the Society for the Study of the Buddha-dharma and renamed Won Buddhism in 1948, it is today one of South Korea's recognized major religions, with a headquarters temple complex at Iksan and congregations on several continents.
dossier.cosmos: Ultimate reality is Il-Won, the One Circle — identified in doctrine with the Dharmakaya Buddha, the mind-seal of all buddhas and sages, and the original nature of every sentient being — enshrined as a plain black circle where other Buddhist halls place a statue. The inherited Buddhist economy of karma and rebirth is retained, and every existence is understood as sustained by the Fourfold Grace of Heaven and Earth, Parents, Fellow Beings, and Laws. The founding motto reads the machine age itself as a cosmic turning: as material civilization undergoes its great opening (gaebyeok), a corresponding great opening of spirit must be cultivated by human effort.
dossier.practice: The signature discipline is Timeless and Placeless Son — Zen practice unfixed from the meditation hall and carried into farming, trade, housework, and speech — paired with seated meditation, chanting, and study at regular dharma meetings; ethics are framed as requiting the Fourfold Grace rather than performing ritual debt. The clergy (kyomu) includes women on formally equal footing, and the order runs universities, hospitals, and charities as an extension of its doctrine of engagement; Sotaesan's first sacred acts were themselves economic — a savings union and a tidal-land levee built with his first disciples.
dossier.canon: The Wonbulgyo kyojon, comprising the Jeongjeon (Principal Book, Sotaesan's own doctrine) and the Daejonggyeong (his collected discourses); the standard scholarly English rendering is Bongkil Chung's 'The Scriptures of Won Buddhism' (2003).
dossier.reflects: Modernization from inside a tradition: the deliberate redesign of contemplative religion — icon, monastery, and clergy all rebuilt — so that the whole discipline fits a layperson's working day.
sources: Bongkil Chung, 'The Scriptures of Won Buddhism: A Translation of the Wonbulgyo kyojon with Introduction' (2003)
Michael Pye, 'Won Buddhism as a Korean New Religion', Numen 49 (2002)

links.cite: Bongkil Chung, 'The Scriptures of Won Buddhism: A Translation of the Wonbulgyo kyojon with Introduction' (2003)
